mnemonics.title: I Find Dogs Can Be A Fun Friend
mnemonics.content: I (int) Find (float) Dogs (double) Can (char) Be (bool) A (array) Fun (for future types) Friend - helps remember basic C++ data types in order of memory size: int, float, double, char, bool

mnemonics.title: Please Excuse My Dear Aunt Sally
mnemonics.content: Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication, Division, Modulus, Addition, Subtraction - Order of operator precedence in C++ expressions

mnemonics.title: Variable Naming Rule - C CAN
mnemonics.content: C - Case sensitive (num and Num are different), A - Alphanumeric allowed (must start with letter or underscore), N - No keywords allowed, N - No spaces or special characters (except underscore)

mnemonics.title: ARITHMETIC Operators - PAM
mnemonics.content: P - Pre/post Increment/Decrement (++/--), A - Assignment (=), M - Mathematical (+, -, *, /, %) - remember these are the core arithmetic operators

mnemonics.title: Logical Operators - A N O
mnemonics.content: A - AND (&&), N - NOT (!), O - OR (||) - three essential logical operators in C++

mnemonics.title: Comparison Operators - TALE
mnemonics.content: T - Two equal signs for comparison (==), A - Above/Below (<, >), L - Less/More or Equal (<=, >=), E - Exclamation mark for Not Equal (!=)

acronyms.usage: Whole numbers without decimals. Use for counting, indexing, and whole number calculations. Example: int age = 20;
Numbers with decimal points (single precision). Use for scientific calculations requiring decimals but not extreme precision. Example: float price = 99.99f;
Numbers with decimal points (double precision). Use when more precision is needed than float. Default for decimal literals. Example: double pi = 3.14159;
Single character enclosed in single quotes. Stores ASCII values. Example: char grade = 'A';
Stores true (1) or false (0). Used in conditions and logical operations. Example: bool isPass = true;
Used for functions that don't return a value. Also used for generic pointers. Example: void display() { cout << 'Hello'; }
Compiler deduces variable type from initialization. C++11 feature. Example: auto x = 10; // deduces int
Makes a variable read-only after initialization. Cannot be modified. Example: const int MAX = 100;

visualTips.visualization: Imagine different-sized boxes: int is a small box (4 bytes), double is a large box (8 bytes), char is a tiny single-slot box (1 byte). The compiler allocates the right-sized box based on data type.
Picture a warehouse with named storage units. Each unit (variable) has a label (name), holds specific contents (value), and has a defined size/type. The label helps you find and access the contents.

Think of operators as verbs performing actions: + (add), - (subtract), == (compare if equal), && (both conditions true). Just like grammar, operators connect your data (nouns/variables) to create meaningful instructions.

Draw a memory map as a street: each house (variable) has an address (memory location), a house number (variable name), and rooms of different sizes (data types). The compiler assigns the right number of rooms.

Picture stairs: () and [] at top (executed first), then unary operators, then multiplication/division, then addition/subtraction, then comparisons, then logical &&, then logical ||, then assignment at bottom.

analogies.analogy: Like containers in a kitchen: small bowls (char for single items), medium plates (int for whole numbers), large serving dishes (double for large decimals). Each container holds different quantities and types of food.

Like labeled jars in a pantry. The jar label is the variable name, what's inside is the value, and the jar size represents the data type. You can change what's inside (value) but the label stays the same.
Like putting a note in a mailbox. The note says '25' (value) and goes into the 'age' (variable) mailbox. The '=' operator is the mail carrier delivering the value to the variable.
Like a judge comparing two people. == asks 'Are they equal?', != asks 'Are they different?', < asks 'Is first smaller?', > asks 'Is first bigger?'. The judge always answers true or false.
Like planning activities: && requires BOTH conditions (rain AND weekend = stay home), || requires EITHER condition (rain OR holiday = no school), ! flips the condition (NOT raining = go outside).
Like elevator buttons: ++ goes up one floor (increments by 1), -- goes down one floor (decrements by 1). Pre (++x) means press button before entering, post (x++) means enter then press button.

Like converting currency at exchange. You have $10 (int) but need euros (double). The exchange booth (cast operator) converts your money to the new type. Explicit casting is like requesting the conversion yourself: (double)10.

Like different rooms in a house: main hall (global scope - everywhere), bedroom (function scope - inside main only), closet (block scope - within {} only). What you have in your bedroom stays in your bedroom.
